title: Deaths from volcanic eruptions
subtitle: Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.
caption:
Deaths from volcanic eruptions, World, 1570 to 2024
Includes direct deaths, in addition to secondary impacts such as a tsunami or earthquake triggered by an eruption. Due to data availability, recent data will be more complete than older historical estimates.

The vertical bars display the number of deaths attributed to volcanic eruptions worldwide for each year between 1570 and 2024, with the vertical axis scaling from 0 to 60,000. The data presents a distribution characterized by long periods of low mortality punctuated by isolated, extreme spikes. The highest visible value occurs in 1815, where the bar reaches the 60,000 mark. The second-highest value appears in 1902, reaching just above 40,000. A third major peak is visible in 1883, with deaths recorded between 30,000 and 40,000. Another significant event is shown in 1994, where the bar extends past the 20,000 line. A smaller but distinct cluster of activity is visible in the late 1700s, specifically around 1792, where deaths exceed 10,000. In contrast, the years between 1600 and 1700 show consistently low bars, mostly remaining under 5,000. The period from 1950 to 2024 shows frequent activity, but most bars remain below the 5,000 threshold, with the exception of the 1994 spike. The chart shows that while volcanic activity results in fatalities across centuries, the total death count in any given year is typically low, with the aggregate total heavily influenced by a small number of catastrophic events. The data shows a pattern where the majority of years record minimal losses, while a few specific years account for the highest mortality figures on the timeline.
